author: @RonRefiner
text:
I have some keyboard mylars and I tried this method on them and it works. Thanks. However, I do have a few questions and hopefully you can help me out.

No#1. Since I have sodium hydroxide in powered or pellet form, how much water do I use to know I have the correct concentration to do, for example, 1 lb of mylars at one time?

No#2. Since I didn't measure the sodium hydroxide and water ratio, with the right mixture, how long should it take to strip the silver off of a 1 lb of mylars?

It stripped some of the silver off the same day, but, it took days later, to almost a week to completely strip the mylar.

No#3. Since the silver will be in metallic form, after filtering the solution, can I store the liquid and use it later on for more mylars?

I've been refining silver for about 2 years now, and I happened to come across this video last week sometime. I have been using Nitric Acid, but it's too dangerous, but, the problem is that it's too expensive to use for 1 lb batches. From what I've learned, it'll take 1.17ml of Nitric Acid to dissolve 1 gram of silver. So, if I want to get 1 oz silver I'd need at least 36.38ml of the nitric, along with the same amount of Sodium Peroxide to make the solution. Now, to get 1 oz of silver from keyboard mylars, you'll need at least 2 lbs of mylars.

The solution doesn't soak all the mylars (72.77ml of solution) at one time, which makes using the nitric too time consuming too, although it'll strip each mylar in a matter of seconds. I also noticed that when I add heat to the nitric/peroxide solution, it slowly evaporates the solution away.

Anyway, I give you 2 thumbs up for this demonstration because I've been looking for an alternate way to strip my mylars. I've tried the incineration method, but I recovered half than the nitric method.

Again, this method can help me do all my mylars in a fraction of the time and cost, compared to using the nitric acid.

author: @ИгорьЖук-г8ы
text:
hello 
Please tell me you used the exposed (used) film or unexposed? As far as I know in the unexposed film strip contains silver bromide. And if unexposed movie film pour sodium hydroxide is precipitated winning silver bromide?
I poured cine film unexposed household bleach - an aqueous solution of sodium hypochlorite (15-30%) and stabilizer (I think it's sodium hydroxide, 5%). Cinematographic film became transparent. In precipitate fell gray matter, please tell me what is the matter.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Cheers mate. I solved the problem by buying a new camera and mike. The quality of later videos is much better

author: @buynow8888
text: This was an extremely useful video -- thank you. As others have mentioned the audio, you might have had the mic gain (volume) up far, been too close, or had alot of hard, reflective surfaces bouncing the sound back into the mic from different directions. I would use an audio editor program as well. Many, many of the folks who post vids would benefit from editing. If nothing else you can filter out all/most of the background noise from your audio.
